claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me about each of them and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 8 years old, so definitely under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great! Thanks for letting me know about Alex. Since he's 8 years old and under 16, he automatically qualifies in terms of age. Let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - at 8 years old, Alex wouldn't be in paid employment, so this next check should be straightforward.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

I'd assume at 8 years old, Alex isn't working, so the answer would be **yes** - but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex isn't working at all. He's just 8, so he's at school full-time!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Alex is at school full-time and not working, so we can move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

This means he's not claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name. At 8 years old, this would be very unusual, so I'd expect the answer is **yes** (he's not receiving these benefits) - but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's only 8, so no, nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly as I'd expect for an 8-year-old! Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This next question is about apprenticeships, which again won't apply to Alex at his age.

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is 8 years old and at school, he's clearly not in any kind of apprenticeship. So the answer would be **not in an apprenticeship** - please confirm this is correct.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - no, Alex isn't in any apprenticeship. He's just in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is in primary school, so no apprenticeship concerns there. Let's move on to check about your residency status.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for at least 3 continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (and not claiming certain means-tested benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? For most people, it's simply option 1 - **normally living in the UK**.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. So option 1 applies to me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these criteria:

1. **Living with Alex** - Alex lives with you
2. **Contributing to Alex's upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You pay maintenance for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Since Alex lives with you, you're clearly responsible for him. Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This could be another parent, a partner, or another family member. Please let me know:

- **No one else is claiming** for Alex
- **Someone else is claiming, but we've agreed** that I should be the one to claim instead
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim for Alex

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's current living situation.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **Normal living** - Alex is living at home with you (the usual situation)
- **In hospital** - Alex is currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Alex is currently in local authority care
- **Fostered** - Alex is currently in a fostering arrangement

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex does live with me at home, but he has been in local authority care for a couple of months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I want to make sure I understand correctly - is Alex **currently** in local authority care right now, or has he been in care in the past but is now back living with you at home?

This distinction matters for determining the next steps in the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's currently in care, but he does spend time at home with me - he comes home on the weekends to stay with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for explaining that. Since Alex is currently in local authority care (even though he spends weekends with you), I need to check a few more details about this situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is helpful information. Since Alex comes home on weekends to stay with you, I need to ask:

**Does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you, OR does he stay at least 2 consecutive nights per week with you?**

You mentioned he comes home on weekends - if he's staying overnight on the weekend (for example, Friday and Saturday nights, or Saturday and Sunday nights), this would likely meet the requirement.

This is important because:
- Normally, Child Benefit can only continue for up to **8 weeks** when a child is in care
- However, there's an exception if the child spends **24+ hours per week** at home, or **2 consecutive nights per week**, or **7+ days in a row** at home

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does spend time at home with me on the weekends - it's definitely more than 24 hours a week when he's here.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's good news! Since Alex spends more than 24 hours a week at home with you, you can still qualify for Child Benefit even though he's in local authority care. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question given Alex's situation:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?**

In other words, is the council covering the costs of Alex's care placement, or are you paying for it yourself?

Generally, if the local council is paying for a child's accommodation or maintenance, you cannot receive Child Benefit for that child. Please let me know whether the council is contributing to Alex's care costs.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying anything towards Alex's care - I'm covering everything myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - since you're covering Alex's care costs yourself and the council isn't paying, this means you meet this requirement as well.

We've now gone through all the eligibility checks, and I have good news for you!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Summary of Your Eligibility:

| Requirement | Status |
|-------------|--------|
| Child's age (under 16) | ✅ Met |
| Child not working 24+ hours/week | ✅ Met |
| Child not receiving own benefits | ✅ Met |
| Child not in disqualifying apprenticeship | ✅ Met |
| Claimant residency (living in UK) | ✅ Met |
| Responsible for child | ✅ Met |
| No other claimant | ✅ Met |
| Care exception (24+ hours/week at home) | ✅ Met |
| Council not paying for care | ✅ Met |

### Important Note About Alex's Care Situation:
Even though Alex is in local authority care, you still qualify because:
- He spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you on weekends
- The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps:
1. **Apply for Child Benefit** online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit) or by post using form CH2
2. You'll need Alex's birth certificate and your National Insurance number
3. You can choose to receive the payments or opt out (to avoid the High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or a partner earn over £60,000)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. His parent lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. Alex lives with his parent, so the responsibility requirement is met. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is currently in local authority care, he spends more than 24 hours per week at home with his parent on weekends, which means the care exception applies. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his parent is covering all costs. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care, the claimant meets the exception criteria because Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home, and the local council is not contributing to Alex's care costs. All other standard eligibility requirements are also met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. His parent lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets residency requirements. Alex lives with his parent, so the responsibility requirement is met. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex is currently in local authority care, he spends more than 24 hours per week at home with his parent on weekends, which means the care exception applies. The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance - his parent is covering all costs. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in local authority care, the claimant meets the exception criteria because Alex spends more than 24 hours per week at home, and the local council is not contributing to Alex's care costs. All other standard eligibility requirements are also met.
